# No Threat Can Force Iran’s Surrender — Trita Parsi & Lt Col Daniel Davis (digest)

**Source:** YouTube — *Daniel Davis Deep Dive* (per closing bumper). Episode title as given: *No Threat Can Force Iran’s Surrender* (Trita Parsi & Lt Col Daniel Davis).

**Watch URL:** *Add exact `https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=…` when pinned.*

**Ingested:** 2026-03-30 (operator paste). **Air / record date:** unknown.

**Participants:** Lt Col Daniel Davis (host); **Trita Parsi**, executive vice president, Quincy Institute (guest).

**Scope:** [work-strategy](../../../../docs/skill-work/work-strategy/README.md) upstream — interview / analysis, not primary reporting. **Not** companion Record; treat numbers, percentages, and war-state claims as **speaker-sourced** until triangulated.

---

## Perceiver — neutral summary (≤200 words)

The segment examines U.S.–Iran hostilities from the **Iranian regime’s likely perspective** under **Trump administration** messaging (e.g. Truth Social posts mixing talk of diplomacy with threats against **energy infrastructure, oil, and desalination**). Parsi argues post–Ayatollah leadership change is **intra-regime** consolidation toward **hawks**, not a new “reasonable regime,” and that **Israeli** strikes removed potential **U.S. negotiating counterparts**. He describes **Iranian** strikes as **systematically** reducing **U.S./Israeli/GCC radar** and **interceptor** effectiveness, improving **penetration rates**, and **pacing** volume for a **longer** conflict; cites **Haaretz** on intercept statistics. He claims **war conditions** have **de facto** eased **Iranian** oil revenue (**price/volume**), extending **sustainability** assumptions from months toward a year, and contrasts **short U.S. political** horizons (**midterms**, **market** pressure, **ground-war** casualty risk) with **Iranian** **existential** framing. On diplomacy, he contrasts **U.S.** statements (**Witkoff**) about **talks** with **Iranian Foreign Ministry** denial of **direct** negotiations and says a **durable deal** would require **U.S. compromise** (e.g. **sanctions relief**, **non-aggression** framing), not **maximalist** “15-point” style demands. Davis raises a possible **Gulf islands** (**Abu Musa**, **Tunbs**) **escalation/narrative** tying **Emirati** claims to a wider **coalition** story. **Quantitative** claims (**oil** prices, **“70%”** damage, timelines) are **not** verified here.

---

## Strategy hooks (for lenses / STRATEGY §IV)

| Thread | One line |
|--------|----------|
| **Escalation geometry** | Symmetric **counter-targeting** (e.g. **desalination**, **grids**) vs one-sided **terror**. |
| **Deterrence credibility** | Whether **U.S. military threat** against **Iran** retains leverage after a **costly** war — speaker thesis. |
| **Energy / Hormuz** | **Strait** closure, **Brent/WTI** levels, **“paper” vs physical** oil price — **market** vs **geopolitical** narrative. |
| **Time horizons** | **Elections**, **base** support via **media**, vs **Iranian** pacing — **attrition** comparison. |
| **Coalition / GCC** | **Oman/Qatar** bilateral deals vs **unified GCC** line on **island** dispute — **messaging** vs **military** utility. |
| **Diplomacy vs theater** | **Messages** vs **negotiations**; **definitions** of “talking.” |

**Triangulate:** Responsible Statecraft piece (speaker attributes argument on **radar** / **interception** to an author named “Jennifer” — surname unclear in ASR). **JP Morgan** regional **oil/product** chart and **Powell** **debt** comments referenced on-screen — cite primary if used in downstream copy.

## Full transcript

*Light cleanup: obvious ASR fixes (names, **Strait of Hormuz**, **Haaretz**, **Emiratis**, **island** names). Substantive wording unchanged.*

---

**Host:** President Trump has issued basically a new ultimatum to Iran. He continues to say we're winning this war and diplomacy is leading the way. Or is it? We've talked a lot this morning about what the U.S. side is doing, the military side, and even what things look like in Europe. But now we want to look at the other side of this equation because you can't understand at all what's going on with the U.S.–Iran war unless you understand what things look like from the other side here.

And we're going to talk today to one of our favorite guests, **Trita Parsi**, who is the executive vice president of **Quincy Institute** and somebody who had some dealings and negotiations with the **2015 JCPOA** that was developed over a painful long period of time, wrote a book on that which I found utterly fascinating. So he has got some great insights into what is and isn't possible on the Iranian side and we're looking forward to getting into that today.

**Trita**, welcome to the show as always.

**Parsi:** Great to be with you again.

**Host:** Well, listen, let's just start off right off the bat with the **Truth Social** that Trump put out at 7:30 this morning. Basically saying on the one hand that the United States is in serious discussions with a new and more reasonable regime to end our military operations and great progress has been made so far.

Now, if you just stopped right there, you'd go, "Oh, great. Maybe things are going pretty good." But then, as you and I were chatting before the show, if you're only listening to certain media, you're not going to know about what's really going on. You may be tended to believe that. We're going to get into that part that [the chyron] is highlighting in a second.

But let me just ask you right off the top. Is there a new more reasonable regime in Iran now that the **Ayatollah** has been assassinated?

**Parsi:** He's right in the sense that there's a change, but it's a change **within** the regime rather than a **change of** regime. And the tendency has been that many of the folks that were more moderate in their context have been replaced with more hawkish people.

In fact, the **Israelis** appear to have deliberately targeted people who they thought **Trump** would negotiate with in order to kill off his off-ramps. And as a result, the nature of the current state of the **Islamic Republic** is one that is more hawkish, more bullish, and more confident that their conduct in this war ultimately will bring them to a much stronger position than they were in during the last couple of years of **the Ayatollah's** reign.

So in that sense, yeah, perhaps one can phrase it the way he is. He's very loose in the way he defines things. And I think there may be some conversations or messages being exchanged. I doubt that there's very serious negotiations going on right now. And when I say that, I mean because serious negotiation requires a much much more realistic negotiation strategy and demands rather than the ones that we so far have seen from the administration.

And look, you also have to be — even on our own side — you've got to be realistic about what is and isn't possible and just how much leverage mileage you can get out of rhetoric. **Trump** has lived in rhetoric. I mean, ever since he started running in **2015** when he first announced and then all the way through and everything in between there.

He's done a lot of that even with some of the military stuff, especially in the **Venezuela** operation. And he has been pretty successful at some of that. It seems to have run into a buzzsaw here actually. And [if you can] put that **Truth Social** back up because this seems to me that some of the teeth on that saw are starting to break off because now he says, "We will conclude that our lovely **Strait of Hormuz** isn't opened up, we will conclude our lovely state in Iran by blowing up and completely obliterating all of their electric generating plants, oil wells, cargo islands, and maybe even all desalinization plants."

Just first of all, is that going to scare Iran to make him say, "Oh, snap, man. We better make some action here pretty quick. We better open up the **Hormuz** and give **Trump** what he wants."

**Parsi:** I mean, look, if the **Iranians** could be scared into surrendering, that would have happened before the war started.

I think from their standpoint they're feeling confident, perhaps too confident, that they have the ability to strike back very hard in manners that will be more painful to the **U.S.** side and to the **Israeli** and the **GCC** side than it will be to the **Iranians**. So for instance, yes, it will be devastating for the **Iranians** to have their desalination plants destroyed.

But **Iran** has actually more natural water than the **GCC** states do. So if the **Iranians** then retaliate by targeting the desalination plants in the **GCC** states, that will create a much graver disaster for them. The same is true for **Israel**. Same thing is also true for **Israel's** power grids, etc. They're more sensitive and vulnerable than the **Iranian** ones are.

So whatever escalatory path **Trump** has been looking at, the **Iranians** appear to be able to match that and make sure that the **U.S.** or its allies end up paying equal, if not even a higher cost. So, I don't see an escalatory path out of this debacle for **Trump**. He should be looking for **de**escalatory paths out of this debacle rather than escalatory paths.

**Host:** Well, that's what he should be doing, but it doesn't seem that he is doing that. And he keeps on talking about this whole issue about negotiations that are going on. And I'm going to touch on that in a second. But this right here actually was put on **Fox News** a few minutes ago, put out by **Caroline Levitt** saying, "Hey, you know what? We're keeping on winning on military. So far, we've continued to obliterate **Iran's** defense industrial base with nearly **70%** of their missile drone and naval production facilities and shipyards damaged or destroyed so far."

But then, let me ask you, your understanding — is that the case? Because those are things on the surface. Yet we see, as this example here with **[Yasin / granite fortress]**, that many of the facilities have been put underground into mountains like this one here. And so, the question is, have we really gotten **70%** of them or has it been **70%** of what we can see on the ground?

**Parsi:** I think it certainly is closer to the latter than the former. And also, we can just take a look at how the **Iranians** are performing in this war. They're going at this very systematically.

They have taken out a lot of the radar systems of the **U.S.** and **Israel**. And as a result, the reason why fewer **Iranian** missiles nevertheless have a much higher penetration rate than what they did before is not just because of **Israel** running out of interceptors, but rather also because the radar systems are being blown up and as a result the **U.S.** and **Israel** are not seeing these missiles early enough to be able to intercept them.

This is an argument that **[Jennifer — surname unclear in audio]** is doing in **Responsible Statecraft** today. A great article that I recommend everyone to read because it shows again that it isn't sufficient for us to just run around with some statistics about the number of places we've hit etc. That's not what ultimately matters because not every site is of equal importance.

And the **Iranians** seem to have been going at this very strategically and making sure that they're making both the **GCC** states and the **Israelis** extremely vulnerable and not just relying on them running out of interceptors, but going after other things that essentially makes it more difficult for the **United States** to actually launch major offensive operations rather than just the defensive operations.

**Host:** And what is going to be the prospect that we can bludgeon the **Iranian** side into submission here because obviously whether it's **70%** of the facilities above ground whatever there's no doubt that we've inflicted tremendous amounts of damage and continued to do so — in the **Iranian** calculation as best you can understand would they have foreseen this level of attacks for an extended period of time and do they think that they can endure this for an extended period of time — not just can keep firing missiles, but can they keep absorbing the receiving missiles?

**Parsi:** So early in the war, it seemed like their expectation and their preparation was that they would be able to withstand a war for about **30** — sorry, about **three months**. Now, I don't know if they're exaggerating or if something has changed in the equation, but you're seeing more and more signaling that they believe that they can withstand this for at least a **year**.

Part of the reason why that may be is because they're actually raking in much more money as a result of them controlling the **Strait of Hormuz** and the oil prices having gone up as much as they have. They're actually selling more oil than they were before and they're selling at almost **three times** the price than they were before.

So, you know, paradoxically, the war has ended up becoming a sanctions relief **de facto** for the **Iranians**. So these factors all coming together, it appears to me that they have also shifted towards a longer strategy. They're not shooting as many missiles at **Israel** any longer, but they're nevertheless getting through at a larger number.

And according to **Haaretz**, they're getting through **80%** of the time, which is quite dramatic. And I think they probably could be shooting far more right now, but they don't want to because they're pacing themselves for this going into a longer period of time.

And I think at the end of the day, despite the fact that they're taking huge amount of hits, they do have a sustainability on their end that probably is superior to that of the **Israelis**.

But also, more importantly, they don't have to outlast **Trump** or play the long game. They just need to play the longer game. They just need to outlast him for **one week**. And **Trump's** ability to withstand this is not for a year. I mean, we have **midterm** elections in just **six or so months**. So, his ability to continue this to me does not appear to be more than **three or four, five weeks** at this point.

So, and it seems quite clear to me that the **Iranians** can withstand themselves to that.

**Host:** Right. And there's a lot of physical evidence to support that contention. As a matter of fact, you've got **JP Morgan** published this right here that showed that these regions of the different regions of the world are going to start to have serious ramifications with petroleum products raging everywhere from **April 15th** — I'm sorry, **April 1st** through the **April 20th** depending on what part of the world you're talking about, but those things are — I mean that's practically tomorrow. You have issues with food because of a lot of the fertilizers and other kinds of feed stocks that are also blocked out that are starting to cause problems everywhere especially in places that can least afford it.

And then here this one by **Jerome Powell** just an hour ago. He's saying listen our national debt is growing substantially faster than the economy says and it is not sustainable. It will not end well if we don't do something fairly soon. Now, I know **President Trump** and **Powell** aren't exactly pals, but **Powell** has been pretty sober in his assessments here. When you're looking, we're already saying we need to add another **$200 billion** in a supplemental for this war. We're trying to get **$1.5 trillion**, some crazy number for next year, too. At the time, the price of oil is going up and it's only going to keep going up. I think it's at **112** right now for **Brent** crude, **103**, I think, for **WTI**. And those things are only going to continue to go farther north. Does **Trump** have even **three or four months** to wait?

**Parsi:** Yeah.

I think that's a great question and I can tell you this as well. If he goes in with **ground troops**, I think those **three to four** weeks will shrink down to **five days** perhaps at most. If the ground troop invasion in and of itself just ends his path of being able to get out of this war while still having some sort of a convincible narrative of victory.

I think it's also very important to understand the **paper price** of oil is not where it should be. You have the **actual** price of oil that probably is closer to **$150**, but the paper price because of manipulation, because of **Trump's** oral interventions, which are to me shockingly effective. I mean, that the tweet that you mentioned in the beginning that just came right before the markets opened up again, right? This is just the way **Trump** is doing it.

Every **Monday morning** he has a tweet of that kind to try to give hope that not only is he winning — he's winning handsomely and this will end relatively soon, he's in control. All to just calm down the markets and give himself more time. They've bought it for some time. I don't think they can buy it for much longer because at some point the **actual** price is going to impose itself on the **paper** price of oil and then things will go way up and then you will see a major shock on gas prices, food prices, all of those different things.

**Host:** Well, yeah. I mean, the evidence certainly supports what you're saying there. And let's take a look at what **President Trump** is trying to do to mitigate further this. You mentioned his comments here, and we've shown a statistic on the price chart. You can see where the **48-hour** deadline that said — you know, that made a big drop and then some other things. There was a **15-point** plan that made it go down for a little while, but every time it starts going back up pretty quick. And this one doesn't seem to have moved much at all down based on his comment here.

But one of the things he's hanging his hat on is that, hey, you know what? I'm still leaving diplomacy open. Here was **Witkoff** a couple of days ago talking about that very thing.

**Witkoff (clip):** The **Iranians**, there are some people there who have denied that we're negotiating. I think we know, everybody in this room knows we are negotiating. It's clear — some ships are coming through. We may have a different definition of negotiating than they do or there may be people within their system today who just don't have the ability to admit it. But we're talking to them.

**Host:** What is your definition of negotiation?

**Witkoff (clip):** Well, my definition of real negotiating would be right here and we don't leave until we're done. And we've offered that and we think there will be meetings this week. We're certainly hopeful for it.

**Host:** And what do you think? I mean, do you think there really will be meetings this week and they're hopeful for it? Because I got to ask, given what happened in **June of 2025**, and given what happened at the start of this war here at the end of **February**, upon what basis would any rational person in the **Iranian** government enter into negotiations with this guy who was the one who led the last round? And he may very well not be with this guy. It may very well be with others in the **White House** who still have some semblance of credibility to be able to negotiate.

**Parsi:** Look, I think there's an element of truth into what **Witkoff** has said there, which is that the **U.S.'s** definition or the **Trump administration's** definition of negotiations may differ from that of the **Iranians** and frankly from that of the rest of the world.

Yes, they're exchanging messages. Are there real negotiations taking place? I doubt it. Should there be? Absolutely.

Should we be happy that the administration at least is trying to look for it or says that it's looking for it? Yes. Are they really looking for it or are they just trying to manipulate the oil markets to give themselves more time? I don't think we have clear evidence yet that there actually is a real effort because a real effort will have to be based on a **compromise** in which the **U.S.** at a minimum is going to have to offer **sanctions relief** in order to end this war rather than these **15 points** that are just nothing more than a demand for **Iran's** capitulation which simply will not happen.

And I'll tell you one more thing that I think is going to come out of all of this. If the war ends under the current circumstances and we don't have some sort of a silver bullet that manages to turn this war around in **Trump's** favor, then one of the outcomes of this war will be that the **credibility of American use of military force** against **Iran** in any future talks will essentially have lost all of its potency.

**Host:** Yeah.

**Parsi:** And this will be a significant difference between what we have seen in the last **20 years** or so. I personally don't believe that the threat of use of force has necessarily been particularly effective or useful in the negotiations that **Obama** did — it was actually the offering of incentives and concessions that generated concessions from the other side rather than threats of military force.

But now we will have a situation in which no sane **American** president will ever try this again because it was not worth it. It was too costly. Did not achieve anything. So these threats no longer will carry that type of credibility and it will make a significant difference on the entire geopolitical equation in the region and I don't think **Washington** has started to wrap its head around what those changes may be and I think I can say with absolute confidence that the current administration hasn't realized that because on the one hand on the **28th of March** there you had **[ASR unclear: "Citco"]** saying that about negotiations and then the very next day **Trump** added his two cents on negotiations. I would only say that we're doing extremely well in that negotiation, but you never know with **Iran** because we negotiate with them and then we always have to blow them up.

Whether it's the **B-2** bombers or just terminating, as an example, the **Iran nuclear deal** done by **Barack Hussein Obama** — probably the worst deal we've ever done as a country. One of the dumbest deals we've ever done. But I terminated — unfortunately otherwise right now they'd have a nuclear weapon. Then we did the attack with the **B-2** bombers and we stopped them from having nuclear and now we had to blow them up again.

And we will — probably I think we'll make a deal with them. Pretty sure whether it's possible or we won't. But we've had regime change. I mean that's about as discombobulated and irrational as it can be in a single sound bite where on the one hand we're negotiating with them but then we had to blow them up and then we negotiated again and then we had to blow them up and oh by the way I got out of the **2015 JCPOA** in **2018** because it was the worst deal and they would have already had a nuclear weapon if I hadn't have gotten out — meaning that the significant constraints would have stayed and somehow that would have turned into — I mean it's irrational. That's the guy that we have that whoever on the **Iranian** side has to negotiate with. How's that going to work?

**Host:** I got to tell you. So, I mean these mixed messages again I think can be explained partly by the president's personality, but also this kind of balancing act of trying to signal how tough and how much we are in control while also saying, "Hey, we're negotiating because if we don't say that, the markets will freak out."

And then the time frame for **Trump** before his own base turns actively against his war dramatically reduces and then he's in real trouble.

So far, just because of the fact that most of them are watching **Fox News** and are getting completely disconnected versions of reality, you still have **70 or so percent** of the base of **Trump** supporting this war.

But that's because they're ill-informed and misinformed by **Fox News**. But once you have gas prices really go up, once the markets no longer are buying this idea that talks are on the verge of success and particularly if you have **American** body bags coming home because of a very dumb decision to go in with ground troops, then I think you're going to see a lot of the support within **Trump's** own base starting to collapse and then he will really be in trouble.

It's important to note that before that happens, he still actually has a chance of getting out of this war with a narrative that his own base will buy that says that he won this war. And they will point to statistics of how much they have blown up, etc. And that will work. But that's now. That's before oil prices have gone up to **$7–8** a gallon. That's before **500 American soldiers** come home in body bags as a result of a stupid invasion of some island in the **Persian Gulf**. Once they pass that **Rubicon**, then I don't know if he ever has any chance of having a narrative of success being manufactured again.

**Host:** But how can even that work? I mean, with the blowing up stuff and statistics, I get that part of it, but you can't have that part of it without having the **Strait of Hormuz** opened up.

And do you see a path under any circumstances **Iran** is going to say, "Yeah, we're going to go back to the **status quo ante**."

**Parsi:** No, they will not go back to the **status quo ante**, but they will open it up if there is a deal on the table that in their view ensures that this war will not happen again, which necessitates **sanctions relief**, several other measures, a **non-aggression pact**.

If all of that is put together, which I think is a realistic plan to end the war, not one of these **15 points** or whatever the **Iranians** have put out that are just maximalist demands — if you have something like that, I think the **Iranians** would be able to agree to it because that would enable them to translate the success that they've had so far into a new stable situation.

That would be the smart thing for them to do. Could it be that they overplay their hands just like **Trump** did in the beginning of this war? That risk is definitely there as well and we should hope that they don't because it serves our interest to make sure that they do not and that we do not.

**Host:** Yeah.

Well, I would love it if we could get both parties to not be dumb. We'll see what it is. But let's take a look at — here is what was just released by the **foreign ministry spokesman** for **Iran** on the issue of negotiations.

**Iran FM spokesman (clip / translation):** We have had no direct negotiations with **America** so far since the last round of negotiations until now.

The **U.S.** demands have been very excessive, unrealistic and illogical.

The meetings that **Pakistan** has with neighboring countries are well a framework they themselves have established. We have not participated in this framework.

Can it be allowed that **Iran** and **Iranians** be in a position of confronting the illegal aggression of **America** and the **Zionist regime** to use the available capabilities on the soil of regional countries while our armed forces remain silent.

The duty of every authority demands that it respond to such cases with — we have had no direct —

**Host:** Yeah. And I mean, I think part of what he's referring to is what was in that **Truth Social** by **Trump**. They're going to hit all of these energy infrastructure and desalinization plants, etc., which he's reiterating what's already been said that we'll do likewise in the region.

How serious of a threat do you think that it would be for **Iran** to make good on that counter strike?

**Parsi:** Oh, it would be devastating. They clearly have the ability to do so. We saw how quickly they inflicted massive damage on the gas facilities in **Qatar** after the **Israelis** bombed **South Pars** gas field and that is going to take **3 to 5 years** for them to be able to rebuild and reconstitute.

So they have the ability to do so in a very short order. The **U.S.'s** radar systems are already in a bad shape. You've seen that interceptors are running low, not just in **Israel**, but in the **GCC** states as well. We've seen that the **Iranians** have been using decoys in order to cause the **Emiratis** to waste a lot of their interceptors.

So I think those threats absolutely need to be taken very very seriously. I think this is part of the reason why **Trump** hasn't done any of these things yet. I mean, he threatened before to strike at the power grids and then he backed off. He's threatened before that he would actually blow up the oil facilities at **Kharq Island** and then he backed off.

He struck **Kharq Island** but not oil facilities because it's in his interest to make sure that **Iranian** oil flows because if it doesn't oil prices will go up even further. That's part of the reason he already unsanctioned **Iranian** oil on the waters. He needed the prices to come down. So these are the undisputable facts and realities that everyone can see and the **Iranians** can see it as well. That's part of the reason why they have so much leverage right now.

**Host:** And you know, if we just kind of froze it right there. We said, "All right, well, there's some upset people in the **Iranian** government for obvious reasons. You've assassinated their leader and killed a bunch of other people and blown up their country, but they also realized that they can't go on forever like this.

So, they need an off-ramp, too. The **United States** definitely needs an off-ramp for all the reasons you've discussed here. And if that's where we were, then maybe there would be some cautious optimism — maybe some threadbare optimism — but there's a lot of evidence on the ground right now that we're about to change the situation again and this misplaced operation to try and bring more pressure to get submission from the **Iranian** government by having a ground operation.

Now, you posted just about an hour or so ago on **X** a supposition of looking at some of the things that may have been — some of the targets that may have been — some of the issues that we may be facing. Can you tell us a little bit about that?

**Parsi:** Yeah. So, in the last **10 days** or so, we've seen increasing activity both on pro-**Israeli** accounts and **Emirati** accounts that they want to turn this war into about these three **Iranian** islands — **Abu Musa** and the **Lesser and Greater Tunb** which are in **Iranian** control. The **Emiratis** make a claim on them. It's a long-standing dispute that they have on this issue.

And that this would be a way of **liberating** those islands essentially. And you know, some of these islands are strategically located, **Abu Musa** in particular, but I don't see it playing that critical role in enabling the **Iranians** to close the straits.

They're doing so not through the control of that island. They're doing so because of the control through missiles and drones over ships in the region. So I, you know, taking the island, I don't see it dramatically changing that equation.

Could the **U.S.** calculate that we'll take an island and then we'll tell the **Iranians** you get the island back if you open up the straits? I don't see it working out that way because once you've taken an island essentially means that you have a bunch of **American** troops there that are stuck.

The **Iranians** are just going to rain missiles and drones over them and cause massive casualties and eventually the **U.S.** would leave without the **Iranians** opening up the strait. So this is — it would be a huge mistake — perhaps even a bigger mistake than the original mistake of starting this war in the first place.

But I do think that there's a political and a messaging value of this that the administration may be looking at which is so far this has been an isolated war in which an isolated **U.S.** and **Israel** have been attacking **Iran** — an illegal war illegal from **U.S.** law from international law. But if you manage to make it about the islands you can achieve two things.

On the one hand, you can say, "Hey, this is a **U.S.–Arab–Israeli** coalition against **Iran**" and the **Emiratis** would likely be part of this operation. And secondly, what you have seen in the last couple of days is that the **Omanis** and the **Qataris** struck their own deal with **Iran**. Instead of having a unified **GCC** position, they struck their own deal and they have not been attacked by the **Iranians** since.

By making this war now about the islands in which the **GCC** states tend to have their unified position in favor of **Emirates** position, then you're kind of trying to force these countries to get back into the war because it's no longer about how this war started. It's now about the islands, an issue that already has clear positions for all of the **GCC** states.

Even if that succeeds, however, from a messaging standpoint, perhaps there's some value in it. But I don't see how it changes the fundamental equation of how do you actually win this war.

**Host:** So we're back to square one.

**Parsi:** Yeah. And I know you're just about out of time here. So in one minute I'll give you a really easy question to end on. Who can sustain this war the longest in a war of attrition? **U.S.**, **Israel**, or **Iran**?

**Parsi:** As it looks right now, it is the **Iranians**. That is not to say that they're not taking huge hits. It is not to say that they are, you know, enjoying this or doing well. Not at all. They're massive civilian casualties, etc. But for them, this was existential.

For **Trump**, this was about his ego. That equation favors the **Iranians**.
